title: Offline RL for Dynamic Pricing
recommendation: For low-risk pricing research, start with conservative fitted Q iteration on a logged-policy dataset and keep an online tabular reference only as a ceiling, not as the deployment default.

title: RL for Supply Chain Routing under Uncertainty
recommendation: For uncertain supply chains, treat routing as a success-and-risk problem, not a shortest-path problem. Report stockout or disruption violations alongside reward.

title: Multi-Agent RL for Traffic Light Grids
recommendation: For CPU traffic-control benchmarks, a centralized joint-action tabular baseline is enough to compare coordination strategies before investing in heavier simulators or neural MARL stacks.

title: Reward Shaping in Resource Allocation
recommendation: When studying reward hacking in energy control, publish at least one sparse baseline and one shaped baseline. The delta is often more informative than the raw best score.

title: Execution Algorithms in Algorithmic Trading
recommendation: Use TWAP-style heuristics as baselines, but score learned execution policies on both realized reward and completion rate. A strategy that looks cheap but fails to finish is not competitive.

title: Safe RL in Chemical Processes
recommendation: For constrained process control, report a composite safe score or a two-axis reward-versus-violations table. Pure reward ranking hides unsafe policies.

title: Curriculum Learning for Job-Shop Scheduling
recommendation: If compute is limited, start here. Curriculum scheduling gives a clean research story, cheap runs, and a meaningful comparison between direct and staged learning.

title: Hierarchical RL for Long-Horizon Logistics
recommendation: If the route horizon is long, include at least one macro-option or action-filter baseline. Flat Q-learning alone understates the value of hierarchy.

title: Meta-RL for Rapid Market Adaptation
recommendation: When claiming fast market adaptation, separate no-adaptation transfer from short-horizon adaptation. Otherwise the comparison hides whether the initialization is actually useful.

title: RL for HVAC Energy Optimization
recommendation: Benchmark HVAC policies on both cost and comfort. If the report only shows one scalar reward, add at least one explicit comfort-violation column.

title: Safe RL with Deterministic Envelopes
recommendation: If a domain has hard safety envelopes, implement the envelope explicitly and compare shielded versus unshielded learning. That ablation is the real result.

title: Sim-to-Real Transfer for Minimal Binaries
recommendation: If deployment space is tight, log the controller footprint and compare domain randomization against short real-world fine-tuning. That trade-off is often the practical decision point.

title: Offline RL for Hardware Resource Allocation
recommendation: For cluster-control logs, conservative fitted Q iteration is a strong first offline baseline because it exposes the value of being pessimistic on under-covered state-action pairs.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Planning
recommendation: If you claim a neuro-symbolic benefit, include the exact symbolic planner, the plain learner, and the hybrid learner in the same table. Anything less leaves the mechanism ambiguous.

title: Multi-Agent Pathfinding Optimization
recommendation: Always include a reservation-style heuristic in MAPF studies. It is simple, strong, and clarifies whether the learning method is discovering genuine coordination.
